docs.title: Home
docs.text: This conceptual tool is based on the framework developed by Patricia Hill Collins and Sirma Bilge (Collins/Bilge 2020; Collins 2019). It provides a set of guiding, methodological, and self-reflexive questions for each of the six core concepts of the intersectional paradigm. These questions can be used to critically assess existing (quantitative) intersectional analyses as well as to design future research projects, supporting theory-driven methodological decision-making throughout the research process. By incorporating a self-reflexive dimension, the tool also encourages critical engagement with positionalities, institutional conditions, and intersectional power relations in knowledge production. The tool aims to support a more theoretically grounded, critical, reflexive, and social justice–oriented approach to (quantitative) intersectional research. Brief introductions to each core concept and its role within the intersectional paradigm are provided in the corresponding notebooks throughout the tool. For a detailed discussion of the development of the framework, its theoretical foundations, and methodology see the accompanying paper: Download paper.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
"Complex questions may require equally complex strategies for investigation." Collins (2019, 47)

To capture the complex dynamics of power and inequality, intersectional research embraces multiperspectivity. Collins describes the combination of different methodological approaches as using different “lenses” (2019, 47), each sharpening some aspects of complexity while leaving others blurred. Intersectional methodological reflection should therefore not aim to seek a single method that can capture all dimensions of complexity at once (Cole 2009, 176), nor at establishing a normative hierarchy among them (Collins 2019, 250). Therefore, the guiding and methodological questions on complexity encourage researchers to reflect systematically on the specific focus of a project.

I propose distinguishing five interdependent dimensions of complexity. The first three draw on Leslie McCall’s (2005) influential typology 1: intercategorical, intracategorical, and anticategorical approaches.

1. The intercategorical approach increases complexity by adding more categories and their intersections. It offers a bird’s-eye view that reveals overarching patterns (McCall 2005, 1786pp). 2. The intracategorical approach focuses the heterogeneity within a social position (e.g. women) that emerges from intersections with other categories (e.g. race). It opposes homogenization and specifically draws attention to marginalized constellations of social positions that are often overlooked (e.g. Black Women) (McCall 2005, 1773, 1781). 3. Anticategorical approaches emphasize the fluidity and social construction of categories (McCall 1773; Else-Quest/Hyde 2016b, 328). They are closely connected to the idea of meta-categorical complexity which adopts a processual approach to categorization (Emmerich/Hormel 2013, 217). These can be expanded by two additional aspects: positional complexity and multilevel complexity.

4. Positional complexity – focusing on the diversity and differentiation of social positions within one category (e.g. gender identity beyond the binary)2. 5. Multilevel complexity – acknowledging that intersecting processes operate across different societal levels 3.

A research project guided by the intersectional paradigm not only faces the complexity of its object of study and its analysis, but also the complexity of implementing intersectional praxis throughout the research process. Such an approach involves complex processes and designs, such as participatory elements, self-reflection, complex operationalization, or sampling strategies.

Given this background, the self-reflexive questions on complexity in knowledge production support critical reflection on the individual and institutional responsibilities for enabling complexity within intersectional research processes.

Notes
  1. McCall (2005, 1784) originally assigns typical methodological approaches to the dimensions of complexity - with quantitative methods being conceptualized as following an intercategorical approach. In contrast, this approach uses McCall's distinction as a heuristic tool to examine how all the different dimensions of complexity can be focused within quantitative intersectional approaches. ↩
  2. The associated methodological challenges are not unique to intersectional research but are already familiar from what Hancock (2007, 64) calls “unitary approaches”. However, they remain relevant and are amplified as complexity increases within intersectional analysis. ↩
  3. This dimension draws on theoretical work on intersectional multilevel analysis (e.g. Winker/Degele 2009) and is also consistent with Crenshaw's (1991, 1245) division into structural, political, and representational intersectionality or the domains of power (Collins/Bilge 2020, 248). ↩

docs.title: Methodological Questions
docs.text:
Complexity | Methodological Questions Click on a question to explore examples and further readings. ↓ Positional Complexity Which/how many social positions e.g. gender identity beyond the binary are included in each category? Methodological Challenges
  • Limitations of the scale level in regression approaches prioritize binary operationalization or one-dimensional polarization.1,2,3,4
  • Predetermined operationalization in secondary analysis.5
Potential Approaches
  • Multidimensional operationalization.6,7,12
  • Applying best practices from unitary approaches.6,8,9,10
  • Fostering intersectionality-sensitive innovation of operationalisation in large databases and official statistics.11,12
Further readings

Inter-categorical Complexity Which/how many categories e.g. gender, race, class, disability... are included in the analysis? Methodological Challenges
  • Overfitting
  • Exponential increase of predictors in regression models with interaction terms1,2,3
Potential Approaches
  • Rejecting the principle of parsimony in intersectional analysis4,5,6
  • Theory-driven or semi-theoretical variable selection7
  • MAIHDA1

Intra-categorical Complexity Which constellations of social positions e.g. Black lesbian women, white non-binary academics, disabled Romnja... become visible at the intersections? Methodological Challenges
  • Small sample size in subgroup analysis.1,2,3,4
  • Asymmetric sample-size distribution.1,2,3,4
Potential Approaches
  • Weighting and shrinking methods.2,6
  • Oversampling.2,7,8,3,9
  • Data pooling.2
  • Reporting non-significant results.5/higher p-value cut-off2,6

Anti-/Meta-categorical Complexity What similarities and continuities exist between differentiated elements? How are boundaries drawn and differences reproduced? Methodological Challenges
  • Strong embedding of categorical thinking.1,2
  • Naturalization of sociodemographic variables (see also core concept ‘power’)
Potential Approaches
  • Discriminatory accuracy.1,3,4
  • Reporting contradictions.5,6
  • Reflection and transparent reporting of classification processes and data used.7,8,6

Multilevel Complexity Which levels of analysis are addressed? At which societal level are the analysed elements situated? Methodological Challenges
  • Limited experience and best practices in empirical research with multilevel approaches; current research often focuses primarily on the individual or identity level.1,2,3,4,5
Potential Approaches
  • Including units of analysis located on different levels.1
  • Multilevel models.2,6,7,8
  • Hierarchical/nested data structures.9

Notes
    The tool provides selected examples of methodological challenges identified in the literature on quantitative intersectional analysis, together with approaches proposed to address them. These examples are derived from the literature analysis underlying the development of the tool. While not exhaustive, they illustrate how the framework can be applied to systematically reflect on methodological concerns. Readers are encouraged to use them as a starting point for further methodological discussion and for expanding the analytical toolkit of (quantitative) intersectional research. Although the examples presented here primarily focus on quantitative approaches, an analogous application of the framework to qualitative research may also prove fruitful.

docs.title: Self-reflexive Questions
docs.text: Complexity | Self-reflexive Questions  What complex demands on research design and research processes arise from applying an intersectional lens throughout the project?    e.g., integrating participatory processes, creating time and space for reflection   To what extent do the actors involved invest resources to enable and sustain complexity within the research process?   e.g., time, emotional labour, funding   Which institutional conditions, constraints, and practices limit the integration of complexity into the research process?  e.g., lack of suitable databases, insufficient funding opportunities, institutional constraints  How are decisions regarding the prioritization, reduction, or simplification of complexity made throughout the research process?   How do researchers' positionalities, intersectional  power relations , and broader institutional structures shape these decisions? docs.title: Relationality
docs.text:
“[...] entities that historically have been conceptualized as separate and oppositional are interconnected and interrelated” Collins & Bilge (2020,198)

Intersectional analysis examines how different systems of power are constructed and maintained through relational, interdependent processes, and how these interactions shape complex, interwoven structures of inequality (Collins 2019, 45). The core concept of relationality is therefore fundamental to the intersectional paradigm (Collins 2019, 48).

“Black women can experience discrimination in ways that are both similar to and different from those experienced by white women and Black men. Black women sometimes experience discrimination in ways similar to white women's experiences; sometimes they share very similar experiences with Black men. Yet often they experience double-discrimination – the combined effects of practices which discriminate on the basis of race, and on the basis of sex. And sometimes, they experience discrimination as Black women – not the sum of race and sex discrimination, but as Black women.”

Crenshaw (1989, 149) Crenshaw emphasizes that the relations between different categories, and the inequalities produced by the ways they interact, can vary.

This raises questions on how to empirically capture, differentiate and analyze the various1 forms and qualities of relational processes.

To systematize how relationality is represented in (quantitative) intersectional approaches, I draw on Collins’ (2019, 226) three modes of relational thinking: ‘addition’2, articulation, and co-formation.

1. ‘Addition’ involves incorporating new elements to explain social inequalities. This follows the core premise of relationality: “[...] entities that historically have been conceptualized as separate and oppositional are interconnected and interrelated” (Collins/Bilge 2020, 198); leading to a shift from an either/or logic to a both/and perspective (Collins 2019, 227; Collins/Bilge 2020, 198). In quantiative intersectional analysis this mode of relational thinking is often put into practice by ‘adding’ predictors to regression models, starting with main effects and extending to interaction terms (Bowleg/Bauer 2016). The goal is to achieve a more precise modelling of the distribution of social inequalities by accounting for multiple systems of power and their joint influence.

2. Articulation focuses on how analyzed elements are dynamically connected to each other – just as a joint (articulatio) links skeletal parts in a way that allows their relationship to change depending on the forces applied (Collins 2019, 232ff., 241). Incorporating articulation into intersectional analysis opens the “black box” of mechanisms by directly examining how intersecting power structures interact to reproduce and shape inequalities, focusing on the internal dynamics of these interactions.

3. Co-Formation describes the idea that categories and their underlying systems of power are mutually constructed in their very meaning and structure (Collins 2019, 241ff.). This mode of relational thinking emphasizes that boundaries between categories blur, as they exist in interdependent and constitutive relations3.

The guiding and methodological questions on relationality systematize how different relational processes and their qualities are empirically captured, depending on the adopted mode of relational thinking. This helps to understand how they can complement one another in a multi-perspective approach (Collins 2019, 250). Turning to the relations between these perspectives, we also reflect on how they are related to one another (e.g. through comparison, integration, synthesis or iterative co-construction) and how tensions, contradictions and inconsistencies are interpreted and addressed.

When applied reflexively, the concept of relationality highlights the relationships between the various actors, institutions, and structures that influence the research process and knowledge production. This may include considerations of inter- or transdisciplinary collaboration (relations between disciplines), participatory research (relations between researchers and participants; see e.g. Moradi 2017, 508), theory–practice transfer and science communication (relations between academia and practice; for best practice see e.g. Aikins et al. 2021), or mixed-methods approaches (relations between qualitative and quantitative methods).

The self-reflexive questions on relationality in knowledge production allow stakeholders to intentionally reflect upon, shape and negotiate such relational processes as part of intersectional research practice.

Notes
  1. Other than quantitative analyses that follow an “intersectionality as testable hypothesis” approach (Hancock 2013), this tool does not differentiate between intersectional and “non-intersectional” (e.g. purely additive) relational processes. Instead, intersectionality is understood as a framework that guides analysis with the aim of making the heterogeneity of relations (e.g. additive, multiplicative, mitigating) visible (see also Evans et al. 2024, 5 for a similar understanding).
  2. ‘Addition’ should not be understood in a strictly mathematical sense, but rather describes the insertion of additional elements into the analysis without making any statements about the mathematical consequences (Collins 2019, 229). To reduce terminological misunderstandings, the term is written in quotation marks.
  3. See also Walgenbach’s (2007) critique of Crenshaw’s metaphor of the intersection.

docs.title: Methodological Questions
docs.text:
Relationality | Methodological Questions Click on a question to explore examples and further readings. ↓ 'Addition' | Adding Categories How might social inequality be modelled more precisely by introducing new categories and their combined effects? Potential Approaches
  • Adding new predictors into multiple regression models.
  • Net-effect analyses: Examining relations between power systems indirectly through the social inequalities they produce (principle of explained variance).1
Methodological Challenges
  • Partial regression coefficients assume that effects of distinct categories can be separated and hierarchized.2,3
  • Main-effect predictors treat effects as parallel yet separable.2,3
  • Main-effect equations can only model purely additive effects; interactions beyond this risk misinterpretation.4
    • Models based solely on main effects are not recommended for intersectional analysis.
  • In net effect analyses (with or without interactions) mechanisms remain in a black box (descriptive approach).5,6,7,8,9
    • hard to develop targeted measures Qualitative heterogeneity of mechanisms producing the same quantitative outcomes remains hidden.
Further readings
  1. Hancock, Ange-Marie (2013): Empirical Intersectionality: A Tale of Two Approaches. In: UC Irvine Law Review 3, 259–296. doi: 10.2139/ssrn.2290294 critically discussing net effect approach to intersectionality
  2. Rouhani, Setareh (2014): Intersectionality-informed quantitative research: A primer. Burnaby: Institute for Intersectionality Research & Policy (Simon Fraser University).detailed description of different analytic strategies including main effect and interaction models
  3. Bauer, Greta R./Churchill, Siobhan M./Mahendran, Mayuri/Walwyn, Chantel/Lizotte, Daniel/Villa-Rueda, Alma Angelica (2021): Intersectionality in Quantitative Research: A Systematic Review of Its Emergence and Applications of Theory and Methods. In: SSM – Population Health 14, 100798. doi: 10.1016/j.ssmph.2021.100798.
  4. Bowleg, Lisa/Bauer, Greta (2016): Invited Reflection: Quantifying Intersectionality. In: Psychology of Women Quarterly 40 (3), 337–341. doi: 10.1177/0361684316654282 illustration of misleading use of main effect models in contrast to including interaction terms
  5. Supik, Linda (2017): Statistik und Diskriminierung. In: El-Mafaalani, Aladin/Scherr, Gökçen/Yüksel, Aladin (Ed.): Handbuch Diskriminierung. Wiesbaden: Springer Fachmedien, 191–207. doi: 10.1007/978-3-658-10976-9_46 discussing terminological challenges of correlation vs. causality that obscure the black box character of descriptive analysis, e.g. ‘explained’ variance
  6. Bolte, Gabriele/Lahn, Ulrike (2015): Geschlecht in der Public-Health-Forschung zu gesundheitlichen Ungleichheiten: Potenziale und Begrenzungen des Intersektionalitätsansatzes. In: GENDER – Zeitschrift für Geschlecht, Kultur und Gesellschaft 7 (2), 51–67. doi: 10.3224/GENDER.V7I2.19312 discussing theoretical considerations of the mechanisms required for descriptive versus analytic approaches
  7. Bauer, Greta R./Scheim, Ayden I. (2019b): Advancing quantitative intersectionality research methods: Intracategorical and intercategorical approaches to shared and differential constructs. In: Social Science & Medicine 226 (April), 260–262. doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2019.03.018 discusses risks of descriptive approaches and argues for analytic approaches, which attend to the causal mechanisms, e.g. to address heterogeneity of outcome and mechanisms

'Addition' | Adding Interactions How can interactions be modelled? How does the model/analysis account for the different ways one variable can change the effects of another? ↳ e.g. summation, amplification, attenuation, reversal of effects... Potential Approaches
  • ‘adding’ interaction terms or intersectional dummy variables into multiple regression models1,2,3
  • Net-effect analyses: Examining relations between power systems indirectly through the social inequalities they produce (principle of explained variance).12
Methodological Challenges
  • Higher-order interactions are difficult to interpret.9,10,11,4
  • Exponentially increasing numbers of predictors increase the risk of overfitting (see intercategorical complexity.
  • In net effect analyses (with or without interactions) mechanisms remain in a black box (descriptive approach).5,6,7,8,9
    • hard to develop targeted measures Qualitative heterogeneity of mechanisms producing the same quantitative outcomes remains hidden.

Articulation How can the black box of mechanisms be opened? How do the power structures become effective, (re)producing inequality? How exactly are the elements dynamically connected? Potential Approaches
  • Analytic approaches to identify mediators: Moderation, mediation, and path analyses 1,2
  • Measuring self-reported experiences of discrimination.4,5,6
  • Complementing quantitative analyses with qualitative exploration of mechanisms.7
Methodological Challenges
  • Operationalizations of discrimination often capture only additive effects.2,6,7
  • Many mechanisms are difficult to observe directly.3,4,5
  • Databases often lack suitable variables to operationalize mechanisms. Further readings
    1. Jackson, John W./VanderWeele, Tyler J. (2019): Intersectional decomposition analysis with differential exposure, effects, and construct. In: Social Science & Medicine 226 (April), 254–259. doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2019.01.033 method development: intersectional decomposition analysis

In: Cultural Diversity & Ethnic Minority Psychology 10, 87–108. doi: 10.1007/s11199-008-9400-z discusses challenges of underlying additive assumptions in operationationalization and analysis of perceived discrimination

Berlin. www.afrozensus.de (30.09.25) spezific operationalization of perceived discrimination for intersection of racism and sexism, mixed method design with qualitative exploration
    Co-Formation How do categories mutually reconstruct one another? How do they shape each other's meanings and structures? Potential Approaches
    • Interdependence analyses: Operationalizing power categories as dependent variables.1,2,3
    • Analysing effect-measure modification (variation of survey instruments by social position)
    • Cross-categorical discrimination indices 4 Methodological Challenges
      • Effects of individual categories can no longer be considered separately.
      • The anti-categorical nature of co-formation challenges conventional categorical thinking.5
      • Very limited methodological experience and best-practice examples exist. Further readings

In: Social Science & Medicine 226 (April), 225–235. doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2018.12.016 method development: cross categorical intersectional discrimination index

          The tool provides selected examples of methodological challenges identified in the literature on quantitative intersectional analysis, together with approaches proposed to address them. These examples are derived from the literature analysis underlying the development of the tool. While not exhaustive, they illustrate how the framework can be applied to systematically reflect on methodological concerns. Readers are encouraged to use them as a starting point for further methodological discussion and for expanding the analytical toolkit of (quantitative) intersectional research. Although the examples presented here primarily focus on quantitative approaches, an analogous application of the framework to qualitative research may also prove fruitful.

docs.text: Relationality | Self-reflexive Questions  How do the relational dynamics between stakeholders shape the research process and knowledge production?   e.g., between theory and praxis, science and activism; across disciplines and in transdisciplinary contexts; between researchers and research participants   How are these relationships intentionally shaped or negotiated as part of intersectional research practice? docs.title: Social Context
docs.text:
“This theme […] informs differences within intersectionality itself” “thinking about social inequalities and power relations […] not in abstract generalizations but in their specific contexts” Collins/Bilge (2020, 196&200)

The intersectional paradigm acknowledges that power structures, inequalities, and their complex relations are context-dependent (Collins/Bilge 2020, 196): The same intersection may exhibit different distributions of social inequality1, different relations between elements of power, and different relevant categories.2

The guiding and methodological questions on social context therefore encourage researchers to examine context effects within datasets (internal contextualization) (Bowleg/Bauer 2016, 338) as well as situating the data within the research’s context (external contextualization) (Else-Quest/Hyde 2016a, 164). However, analyses should consider both differences and similarities across contexts, in order to avoid naturalizing distinctions — particularly between nation states — and to remain attentive to overarching processes (e.g. transnational dynamics) (Collins/Bilge 2020, 195).

Speaking from the standpoint-theoretical understanding outlined above, applying the core concept of social context reflexively can be understood as locating the standpoint of knowledge production within the field of intersectionality itself as well as within broader social contexts. The self-reflexive questions on social context in knowledge production therefore address the disciplinary contexts influencing the analysis (e.g. epistemology, terminology, research protocols, conception of theory) as well as the contexts that have shaped the researchers’ understanding of intersectionality. This includes, for example, academic training, the bodies of literature informing the research process, and experiential knowledge emerging from political engagement, activism, or intersectional praxis (Collins/Bilge 2020, 197; Moradi 2017, 500.).

Notes
  1. For an empirical example, see McCall's (2001) study of wage inequality, which compares different economic contexts in US cities.
  2. In their overview of the reclassification of racialised variables in the US Census, the Pew Research Center (2020) provides a graphic example of how metacategorical and positional complexity depend on the temporal context.
References
    Collins, Patricia Hill/Bilge, Sirma (2020): Intersectionality (Key Concepts). 2nd Edition. Cambridge: Polity. Bowleg, Lisa/Bauer, Greta (2016): Invited Reflection: Quantifying Intersectionality. In: Psychology of Women Quarterly 40 (3), 337–341. doi: 10.1177/0361684316654282 Else-Quest, Nicole M./Hyde, Janet Shibley (2016a): Intersectionality in Quantitative Psychological Research. I.Theoretical and Epistemological Issues. In: Psychology of Women Quarterly 40 (2), 155–170. doi: 10.1177/0361684316629797 Moradi, Bonnie/Grzanka, Patrick R. (2017): Using Intersectionality Responsibly: Toward Critical Epistemology, Structural Analysis, and Social Justice Activism. In: Journal of Counseling Psychology 64 (5), 500–513. doi: 10.1037/cou0000203 McCall, Leslie (2001): Complex Inequality: Gender, Class, and Race in the New Economy. New York: Routledge. doi: 10.4324/9780203902455 Pew Research Center. (2020). What Census Calls Us - A Historical Timeline. pewsocialtrends.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2015/06/st_15.06.11_ multiracial-timeline.pdf (15.09.2025)

docs.title: Methodological Questions
docs.text:
Social Context | Methodological Questions Click on a question to explore examples and further readings. ↓ Internal Contextualization How does the analysis account for context-dependent variations ↳ e.g. variations in distribution of social inequality, variations in meaning,relevance and boundaries of categories/social positions, variations in relational processes... within the data? Methodological Challenges
  • Large datasets or pooled data contain high contextual heterogeneity, which creates a risk of overgeneralization.1,2
  • Secondary data may lack contextual variables.
Potential Approaches
  • Comparative analysis of nested data, e.g. multilevel analysis.3,4
  • Incorporating contextual variables 4 e.g. place or date of data collection
  • Longitudinal pre-post designs e.g. to evaluate organizational equality measures
  • Transparent reporting and reflection on context-specific operationalization 5,1 e.g. time- and country-dependent racialized categorization
Further readings
  1. Bolte, Gabriele/Lahn, Ulrike (2015): Geschlecht in der Public-Health-Forschung zu gesundheitlichen Ungleichheiten: Potenziale und Begrenzungen des Intersektionalitätsansatzes. In: GENDER – Zeitschrift für Geschlecht, Kultur und Gesellschaft 7 (2), 51–67. doi: 10.3224/GENDER.V7I2.19312 highlighting the influence of temporal and sociocultural context on categories and inequality; critiques the pooling of data for reducing context sensitivity

In: Social Science & Medicine 203 (April), 64–73. doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2017.11.011 Evans, Clare R./Borrell, Luisa N./Bell, Andrew/Holmann, Daniel/Subramanian, S. V./Leckie, George (2024): Clarifications on the Intersectional MAIHDA Approach: A Conceptual Guide and Response to Wilkes and Karimi (2024). In: Social Science & Medicine 345 (116898). doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2024.116898 intersectional comperative analysis between schools/neighborhood//poverty context, using MAIHDA

External Contextualization How did the context of data collection shape the data? Which contexts are represented or excluded? What are the implications? To what extent are findings generalizable beyond the immediate context, and what are context-specific particularities? Methodological Challenges
  • Marginalized social positions may be systematically under- or over-represented depending on context.3,4 e.g. inaccessible questionnaires or racial profiling in criminal statistics
  • Representativeness can only be assessed for categories with reference data, but data gaps often exist for marginalized groups 1,2 e.g. on the distribution of gender diversity or the number of people affected by racism
Potential Approaches
  • Transparent reporting on the conditions of data collection3,6
  • Critical reflection on dataset representativeness7: - Specifying reference variables. - Avoiding absolute claims of representativeness.

Wiesbaden: Springer Fachmedien, 191–207. doi: 10.1007/978-3-658-10976-9_46 Highlights how categories and social positions may be differently represented depending on data collection contexts.

docs.title: Social Inequality
docs.text:
“Intersectionality rejects […] notions that normalize inequality by depicting it as natural and inevitable. Instead, intersectionality points to the workings of power relations in producing social inequalities.” Collins (2019, 46)

By making the contingent and dynamic production of inequality visible, intersectional analysis resists its normalization or naturalization (Collins 2019, 46) and challenges narratives that explain social inequality without reference to power dynamics.

However, descriptive approaches tend to leave the mechanisms of inequality in a black box, opening space for alternative explanations that neglect power relations (Bauer/Scheim 2019, 237) (e.g. biologization or meritocratic logics (Collins/Bilge 2020, 38, 203)).

The guiding and methodological questions on inequality support a stronger theoretical grounding of quantitative intersectional analyses in theories of inequality. They encourage engagement with possible underlying mechanisms.

The self-reflexive questions on inequality also entail how dominant narratives of inequality (e.g., meritocratic, biologizing, or culturalist explanations) may shape researchers’ own assumptions, disciplinary traditions, and the ways results are perceived by the audience.

References

Durham: Duke University Press. doi: 10.1215/9781478007098 Collins, Patricia Hill/Bilge, Sirma (2020): Intersectionality (Key Concepts). 2nd Edition. Cambridge: Polity. docs.title: Methodological Questions
docs.text:
Social Inequality | Methodological Questions Click on a question to explore examples. ↓ Objects of Analysis Which aspects of social inequality are constituted as objects of analysis? Methodological Challenges
  • High heterogeneity of dimensions and manifestations of social inequality challenges the development of appropriate methodologies.1
Potential Approaches
  • Economic inequality.
  • Educational inequality.
  • Unequal social participation.
  • Unequal distribution of health and illness.
Mechanisms of Inequality How are the mechanisms that (re-)produce and structure social inequality conceptualized, theoretically framed, and empirically analysed? Methodological Challenges
  • Descriptive approaches (see 'addition') leave mechanisms in a "black box", allowing alternative, non-power-sensitive explanations. 2,3,4
    • Biologization, especially in health research. Meritocratic logics, especially in educational research. Behavior-focused explanations that ignore structural inequalities.
Potential Approaches
  • Making power-sensitive hypotheses explicit in theory, analysis, and interpretation.2,3
  • Explicitly challenging and deconstructing the normalization and naturalization of inequality.2,3
Further Readings
  1. Bauer, Greta R./Churchill, Siobhan M./Mahendran, Mayuri/Walwyn, Chantel/Lizotte, Daniel/Villa-Rueda, Alma Angelica (2021): Intersectionality in Quantitative Research: A Systematic Review of Its Emergence and Applications of Theory and Methods. In: SSM – Population Health 14, 100798. doi: 10.1016/j.ssmph.2021.100798. literature review showing diversity of studied outcomes e.g. health educational or economical inequalities.
  2. Collins, Patricia Hill (2019): Intersectionality as Critical Social Theory. Durham: Duke University Press. doi: 10.1215/9781478007098 discussing meritocratic explanations of inequality
  3. Collins, Patricia Hill/Bilge, Sirma (2020): Intersectionality (Key Concepts). 2nd Edition. Cambridge: Polity. discussing meritocratic explanations of inequality.
  4. Yuval-Davis, Nira (2006): Intersectionality and feminist politics. In: European Journal of Women’s Studies 13 (3), 193–209. doi: 10.1177/1350506806065752 discussing naturalization of inequality.
Notes
    The tool provides selected examples of methodological challenges identified in the literature on quantitative intersectional analysis, together with approaches proposed to address them. These examples are derived from the literature analysis underlying the development of the tool. While not exhaustive, they illustrate how the framework can be applied to systematically reflect on methodological concerns. Readers are encouraged to use them as a starting point for further methodological discussion and for expanding the analytical toolkit of (quantitative) intersectional research. Although the examples presented here primarily focus on quantitative approaches, an analogous application of the framework to qualitative research may also prove fruitful.

docs.title: Social Justice
docs.text:
“Intersectionality’s raison d’être is not simply to provide more complex and comprehensive explanations of how and why social inequalities persist, […] leaving someone else to fix them. […] When it comes to social justice, intersectionality demands […] turning critical analyses into critical praxis.” Collins/Bilge (2020, 202-203)

Intersectional theory and praxis are inherently linked to a commitment to social justice. This normative dimension challenges the traditional boundaries between academic inquiry, activism, and social movements (Collins/Bilge 2020, 47, 202). However, the core concept of social justice has become increasingly contested within the intersectional field (Collins/Bilge 2020, 202), risking the loss of intersectionality’s transformative potential. In quantitative approaches, it is particularly challenged by the dominance of positivist epistemologies and the associated understanding of objectivity (Else-Quest/Hyde 2016a, 159ff.).

The guiding and methodological questions therefore encourage researchers to clarify how concepts such as justice, equality, or equity are theoretically conceptualized, including their underlying epistemological premises. Furthermore, they encourage reflection on how research is positioned in relation to social justice praxis and how these commitments are translated into concrete research practices.

The self-reflexive questions on social justice in knowledge production focus on the ethical and political implications of intersectional research and support the development of social justice–oriented research ethics (for best practice examples see e.g. Ahyoud et al. 2018, 33+38; Aikins et al. 2021, 33–34).

References

Durham: Duke University Press. doi: 10.1215/9781478007098 Else-Quest, Nicole M./Hyde, Janet Shibley (2016a): Intersectionality in Quantitative Psychological Research. Social Justice | Methodological Questions Click on a question to explore examples. ↓ Theoretical Conceptualization How is justice theoretically conceptualized? How are social inequalities normatively evaluated? Methodological Challenges
  • Positivist epistemology emphasizes value-neutrality, challenging the normative dimension of social justice.1
Potential Approaches
  • Providing a clear definition and conceptualization of justice e.g. equity vs. equality vs. justice
  • Naming and acknowledging injustice.
Epistemology and Normativity Which epistemological paradigm underlies the research? What assumptions about objectivity are made? How is the relationship between science and normative/political praxis understood? Methodological Challenges
  • Positivist epistemology emphasizes value-neutrality, challenging the normative dimension of social justice.1
Potential Approaches
  • Reflecting on the epistemological assumptions underlying the research.1
Social Justice Praxis How are research findings disseminated to inform social justice projects? How can the research contribute to actionable interventions or policy recommendations? Methodological Challenges
  • Controversy over whether quantitative research can actively engage with social justice.2,3
Potential Approaches
  • Developing strategies for theory–praxis transfer and dissemination beyond academia.4
  • Empowerment through knowledge (e.g. participatory action research).1,5,6
  • Analysis and evaluation of social justice praxis as an object of inquiry.1

docs.title: Power
docs.text:
“[Intersectional] frameworks that take power relations into account […] raise new questions about global social inequality.” Collins & Bilge (2020, 36)

Intersectional research examines interlocking systems of power and their co-production of social inequalities (Collins 2019, 46; Collins/Bilge 2020, 35, 191). These interwoven structures operate across multiple levels of society, forming a multidimensional matrix of domination. Within this matrix, power is continually negotiated and reconfigured through processes of oppression and resistance (Collins/Bilge 2020, 36, 192).

However, translating this understanding into quantitative methodologies poses significant challenges. Socio-demographic and administrative variables are frequently naturalized and treated as self-explanatory (Supik 2017, 203), while complex, power-sensitive operationalizations are rarely available.

The guiding and methodological questions on power support a stronger theoretical grounding of quantitative intersectional analyses in theories of power.

Moreover, the tool’s The self-reflexive questions on power in knowledge production acknowledge that research itself is embedded in the very hierarchies it seeks to analyze (Collins/Bilge 2020, 194) – on the individual level and within institutional structures.

Critical reflection on how these power relations shape knowledge production is grounded in Black feminist epistemology, which emphasizes that standpoints are systematically structured through intersectional relations of power and that marginalized positions can generate distinctive analytical insights (Collins 2000). Reflexively engaging with and critically challenging these dynamics is therefore situated at the very heart of the intersectional paradigm.

References
    Collins, Patricia Hill/Bilge, Sirma (2020): Intersectionality (Key Concepts). 2nd Edition. Cambridge: Polity. Collins, Patricia Hill (2019): Intersectionality as Critical Social Theory. Durham: Duke University Press. doi: 10.1215/9781478007098 Supik, Linda (2017): Statistik und Diskriminierung. In: El-Mafaalani, Aladin/Scherr, Gökçen/Yüksel, Aladin (Ed.): Handbuch Diskriminierung. Wiesbaden: Springer Fachmedien, 191–207. doi: 10.1007/978-3-658-10976-9_46 Hill Collins, Patricia (2000): Black feminist thought. Knowledge, consciousness, and the politics of empowerment. New York: Routledge.

docs.title: Methodological Questions
docs.text:
Power | Methodological Questions Click on a question to explore examples and further readings. ↓ Operationalization How does the operationalization conceptualize the variables within power dynamics? Methodological Challenges
  • Socio-demographic and administrative variables are often naturalized and treated as self-explanatory.1
  • Databases lack complex power-sensitive variables.
Potential Approaches
  • Linking variables to relevant power structures in theoretical conceptualization and interpretation.
  • Critically reflecting on operationalization, e.g. limits of proxy variables.9,10
  • Considering categorization itself as an exercise of power.7,8
    • Conception of Power What conception of power underlies the analysis? To what extent are oppression and resistance analysed? To what extent are marginalisation and privilege considered? Methodological Challenges
    • Quantitative results are often perceived as objective facts, leading to weak theoretical grounding.2,3
    • Privileged social positions are often treated as the reference group, which can normalize privilege.4,5,6
    • Overlapping constellations of privileged and marginalized social positions are difficult to model and interpret.4
    Potential Approaches
    • Making the understanding of power explicit and building on intersectional theory.11
    • Using MAIHDA to model marginalization and privilege.4
    • Considering alternative coding strategies to make privilege explicit.5
    Further readings
    1. Supik, Linda (2017): Statistik und Diskriminierung. In: El-Mafaalani, Aladin/Scherr, Gökçen/Yüksel, Aladin (Ed.): Handbuch Diskriminierung. Wiesbaden: Springer Fachmedien, 191–207. doi: 10.1007/978-3-658-10976-9_46 criticizing that belonging to a statistical group should not be seen as a sufficient explanation for inequality
    2. Covarrubias, Alejandro/Vélez, Véronica (2013): Critical Race Quantitative Intersectionality: An Anti-Racist Research Paradigm That Refuses to ‘Let the Numbers Speak for Themselves’. In: Lynn, Marvin/Dixson, Adrienne D. (Ed.): Handbook of Critical Race Theory in Education. New York: Routledge, 270–285. doi: 10.4324/9780203155721 critizing lack of theoretical grounding and risk to perceive numbers as natural facts
    3. Bauer, Greta R./Churchill, Siobhan M./Mahendran, Mayuri/Walwyn, Chantel/Lizotte, Daniel/Villa-Rueda, Alma Angelica (2021): Intersectionality in Quantitative Research: A Systematic Review of Its Emergence and Applications of Theory and Methods. In: SSM – Population Health 14, 100798. doi: 10.1016/j.ssmph.2021.100798. systematic literature review showing lack of theoretical grounding
    4. Evans, Clare R./Williams, David R./Onnela, Jukka Pekka/Subramanian, S. V. (2018): A multilevel approach to modeling health inequalities at the intersection of multiple social identities. In: Social Science & Medicine 203 (April), 64–73. doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2017.11.011 developing MAIHDA: a new method with potential to improved analysis of marginalization and privilege
    5. Harari, Lexi/Lee, Chioun (2021): Intersectionality in quantitative health disparities research: A systematic review of challenges and limitations in empirical studies. In: Social Science & Medicine 277 (May), 113876. doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2021.113876 Rreview showing privileged group is typically set as the reference
    6. Bauer, Greta R. (2014): Incorporating Intersectionality Theory into Population Health Research Methodology: Challenges and the Potential to Advance Health Equity. In: Social Science & Medicine 110 (June), 10–17. doi: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2014.03.022 criticizes conceptualizing privilege as the absence of discrimination
    7. Cole, Elizabeth R. (2009): Intersectionality and Research in Psychology. In: American Psychologist 64 (3), 170–180. doi: 10.1037/a0014564 suggest reflexion questions e.g. ‘who is included in the category’ to draw attention to systematic exclusions and power at play in processes of categorization
    8. Else-Quest, Nicole M./Hyde, Janet Shibley (2016a): Intersectionality in Quantitative Psychological Research. In: Psychology of Women Quarterly 40 (2), 155–170. doi: 10.1177/0361684316629797 discusses how power shapes the social construction of social categories
    9. Baumann, Anne-Luise/Egenberger, Vera/Supik, Linda (2018): Erhebung von Antidiskriminierungsdaten in repräsentativen Wiederholungsbefragungen. Bestandsaufnahme und Entwicklungsmöglichkeiten. Berlin: Antidiskriminierungsstelle des Bundes (Ed.). https://www.antidiskriminierungsstelle.de/SharedDocs/downloads/DE/publikationen/Expertisen/erhebung_von_antidiskr_daten_in_repr_wiederholungsbefragungen.pdf?__blob=publicationFile&v=2 (30.09.25) systematic review on discrimination relevant-variables in surveys, discussing potential and challenges of proxy variables especially regarding racism

Antidiskriminierungs- und Gleichstellungsdaten in der Einwanderungsgesellschaft – Eine anwendungsorientierte Einführung Vielfalt entscheidet.     11. Else-Quest, Nicole M./Hyde, Janet Shibley (2016b): Intersectionality in Quantitative Psychological Research: II. Methods and Techniques. In: Psychology of Women Quarterly 40 (3), 319–336. doi: 10.1177/0361684316647953 highlights power-sensitive interpretation and theoretical framing as essential for intersectional analysis
